name: oemof-tools

metadata.name: oemof.tools
metadata.summary: Tiny tools of the oemof project.

documentation:
# Overview

Tiny tools of the oemof project.

- Free software: MIT license

## Installation

```
pip install oemof.tools
```

You can also install the in-development version with:

```
pip install https://github.com/oemof/oemof-tools/archive/dev.zip
```

## Documentation

<https://oemof-tools.readthedocs.io/>

## Development

To run the all tests run:

```
tox
```

Note, to combine the coverage data from all the tox environments run:

```
.. list-table::
    :widths: 10 90
    :stub-columns: 1

    - - Windows
      - ::

            set PYTEST_ADDOPTS=--cov-append
            tox

    - - Other
      - ::

            PYTEST_ADDOPTS=--cov-append tox


```

## Changelog

### 0.4.2 (2022-06-15)

- Add Python support for Python 3.10 and drop support for 3.7
- Move CI from Appveyor and Travis to Github Actions
- Use [Black Code Style](https://black.readthedocs.io) for oemof.tools
- Change default logging level for file logging from DEBUG to WARNING

### 0.4.1 (2021-02-21)

- Fix compatibility problem (by naming submodules in init script)

### 0.4.0 (2020-05-11)

- Move the code of the oemof.tools repository to a stand-alone repository.
- Use cookiecutter to create a wider testing structure and a more standardised
  package structure.
- Switch from nose to pytest
- Remove the version or commit logger
- Add test to increase test coverage

modules.name: oemof.tools.debugging
documentation:
Module contains tools facilitating debugging

This file is part of project oemof (github.com/oemof/oemof). It's copyrighted
by the contributors recorded in the version control history of the file,
available from its original location oemof/oemof/tools/economics.py

SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

classes.name: oemof.tools.debugging.SuspiciousUsageWarning
documentation:
Warn the user about potentially dangerous usage.

Some ways of using `oemof` are not necessarily wrong but could lead to
hard to find bugs if done accidentally instead of intentionally. We
use these warnings, and you can do too ;), in your code to warn users about
these cases. If you know what you are doing and these warnings point you to
things you are doing intentionally, you can easily switch them off.

# Note

TODO: Fix ref!
See {ref}`oemof_tools_debugging_suspicioususagewarningsolph_label` for more
information.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import warnings
>>> warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=SuspiciousUsageWarning)
```

classes.name: oemof.tools.debugging.ExperimentalFeatureWarning
documentation:
Warn the user about use of experimental features.

New modules first go to "experimental" ti highlight their unmature state.
Sometimes, functionality is added to existing code. We use this warning
to warn users in these cases.

modules.name: oemof.tools.economics
documentation:
Module to collect useful functions for economic calculation.

This file is part of project oemof (github.com/oemof/oemof). It's copyrighted
by the contributors recorded in the version control history of the file,
available from its original location oemof/oemof/tools/economics.py

SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

functions.name: oemof.tools.economics.annuity
documentation:
Calculates the annuity of an initial investment 'capex', considering
the cost of capital 'wacc' during a project horizon 'n'

In case of a single initial investment, the employed formula reads:

$$
\text{annuity} = \text{capex} \cdot     \frac{(\text{wacc} \cdot (1+\text{wacc})^n)}     {((1 + \text{wacc})^n - 1)}
$$

In case of repeated investments (due to replacements) at fixed intervals
'u', the formula yields:

$$
\text{annuity} = \text{capex} \cdot           \frac{(\text{wacc} \cdot (1+\text{wacc})^n)}           {((1 + \text{wacc})^n - 1)} \cdot \left(           \frac{1 - \left( \frac{(1-\text{cost\_decrease})}           {(1+\text{wacc})} \right)^n}           {1 - \left(\frac{(1-\text{cost\_decrease})}{(1+\text{wacc})}           \right)^u} \right)
$$

# Parameters

capex

: Capital expenditure for first investment. Net Present Value (NPV) or
  Net Present Cost (NPC) of investment

n

: Horizon of the analysis, or number of years the annuity wants to be
  obtained for (n>=1)

wacc

: Weighted average cost of capital (0\<wacc\<1)

u

: Lifetime of the investigated investment. Might be smaller than the
  analysis horizon, 'n', meaning it will have to be replaced.
  Takes value 'n' if not specified otherwise (u>=1)

cost_decrease

: Annual rate of cost decrease (due to, e.g., price experience curve).
  This only influences the result for investments corresponding to
  replacements, whenever u\<n.
  Takes value 0, if not specified otherwise (0\<cost_decrease\<1)

# Returns

float

: annuity

modules.name: oemof.tools.logger
documentation:
Helpers to log your modeling process with oemof.

This file is part of project oemof (github.com/oemof/oemof). It's copyrighted
by the contributors recorded in the version control history of the file,
available from its original location oemof/oemof/tools/logger.py

SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

functions.name: oemof.tools.logger.define_logging
documentation:
Initialise customisable logger.

# Parameters

logfile

: Name of the log file, default: oemof.log

logpath

: The path for log files. By default a ".oemof' folder is created in your
  home directory with subfolder called 'log_files'.

file_format

: Format of the file output.
  Default: "%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(module)s - %(message)s"

screen_format

: Format of the screen output.
  Default: "%(asctime)s-%(levelname)s-%(message)s"

file_datefmt

: Format of the datetime in the file output. Default: None

screen_datefmt

: Format of the datetime in the screen output. Default: "%H:%M:%S"

screen_level

: Level of logging to stdout. Default: 20 (logging.INFO)

file_level

: Level of logging to file. Default: 30 (logging.WARNING)

log_path

: If True the used file path is logged while initialising the logger.

timed_rotating

: Option to pass parameters to the TimedRotatingFileHandler.

# Returns

str : Place where the log file is stored.

# Notes

By default the INFO level is printed on the screen and the DEBUG level
in a file, but you can easily configure the logger.
Every module that wants to create logging messages has to import the
logging module. The oemof logger module has to be imported once to
initialise it.

# Examples

To define the default logger you have to import the python logging
library and this function. The first logging message should be the
path where the log file is saved to.

```pycon
>>> import logging
>>> from oemof.tools import logger
>>> mypath = logger.define_logging(
...     log_path=True, timed_rotating={'backupCount': 4},
...     screen_level=logging.ERROR, screen_datefmt = "no_date")
>>> mypath[-9:]
'oemof.log'
>>> logging.debug("Hallo")
```

functions.name: oemof.tools.logger.extend_basic_path
documentation:
Returns a path based on the basic oemof path and creates it if
necessary. The subfolder is the name of the path extension.

functions.name: oemof.tools.logger.get_basic_path
documentation:
Returns the basic oemof path and creates it if necessary.
The basic path is the '.oemof' folder in the $HOME directory.
